title: Teacher's Guide_ Spring Days Final
text:
Teacher's Guide: Spring Days
Lesson Guide (Overview)
Basic Info: 
Book Title: Spring Days; 
Level: 1; 
Time: 60 mins
Vocabulary & Phonics Goal: 
Vocabulary: warm, grow, garden, sing, sunshine, outside; Primary Phonics Rule: blend spr-, long a (days) and onset-rime pattern s–un → sun
Key Objectives: 
Students will be able to identify and use the vocabulary words warm, sing, sunshine, and outside; recognize the phonics rules (blend spr-, long a, onset-rime s–un); describe spring using sensory details; answer simple comprehension questions about the text; and use the sentence frame “I like ___.”
Pre-Reading Support
Warm up & Purpose:
Teacher Says: “Today we are going to read a book called Spring Days. Let’s find out what spring feels like. We are also looking for describing words about spring.” Teacher Asks:
 “What can you see in spring? What can you feel or smell?” 
Expected Response: 
Students share sensory observations (e.g., “flowers,” “warm air,” “fresh smell”).
Phonics Focus (Interactive Multi-Step):
Rule Identification: Onset-rime pattern (s–un → sun) and blend spr-, long a (days)
Step 1: Sound Discovery (I Do):
Teacher Says: “Let’s look at the word ‘sun.’ The first sound is /s/, and the rest is /un/. When we put them together, it’s /s/ + /un/ = sun. Let’s say that together: s-un-sun.” Teacher Action: Clap for each sound (/s/ - clap, /un/ - clap) and blend them.
Teacher Says: “Now let’s look at ‘spring.’ The first blend is /spr/, then /ing/ - spr-ing-spring. And ‘days’ has a long a sound: /d/ + /ay/ + /s/ = days.”
Step 2: Word Family Extension (We Do):
Teacher Says: “Let’s practice words like ‘sun.’ If /s/ + /un/ = sun, then /r/ + /un/ = run. Say run with me: r-un-run. Now /f/ + /un/ = fun. Say fun: f-un-fun.” 
Teacher Action: Write “sun, run, fun” on the board and point to each as students say them. 
Teacher Says: “Great! Now let’s try the long a sound with ‘days’—another word with long a is ‘rays.’ Say rays: r-ay-s-rays.”
Step 3: Text Scavenger Hunt (You Do):
Teacher Says: “Open your book to the cover. Look for words that have the /s/ + /un/ sound or the long a sound. Raise your hand when you find one.” 
Expected Response: Students point to “spring” (spr- blend) or “Days” (long a) on the cover.
Step 4: Movement Challenge:
Teacher Says: “When I say a word from our word family (sun, run, fun), jump once for each sound. Let’s start: sun.” 
Teacher Action: Models jumping once for /s/, once for /un/, and once for the blended word. 
Expected Response: Students jump as they segment each word’s sounds.
Vocabulary Preview (Optional):
warm:
Teacher Action: Rubs hands together as if cold, then smiles and holds hands out as if feeling warm. 
Teacher Says: “This is warm. Warm is how we feel when the sun is out in spring. Say warm with me: warm.” 
Expected Response: Students say “warm” and mimic the hand gesture.
sunshine:
Teacher Action: 
Makes a circle with hands above head like the sun. 
Teacher Says: “This is sunshine. Sunshine is the light from the sun. It feels soft on our faces. Say sunshine: sunshine.” 
Expected Response: Students say “sunshine” and make the sun gesture.
garden:
Teacher Action: Pretends to plant a flower. 
Teacher Says: “A garden is a place where flowers grow. Say garden: garden.” Expected Response: Students say “garden” and mimic planting.
During Reading Strategies
Step 1: Picture Walk (Visual Only):
Page 2
Teacher Action: Point to sunshine, smiling child, clear sky.
Teacher Says: Look at Page 2. The sun is shining. The weather is warm and nice. The child looks happy.
Teacher Asks: How is the weather here?
Expected Response: Warm, nice, sunny.
Teacher Confirms/Expands: Perfect! It’s warm and nice. This is spring weather.
Page 3
Teacher Action: Point to flowers, garden, green leaves.
Teacher Says: Look at Page 3. These are beautiful flowers. They grow in a garden.
Teacher Asks: What grows in the garden?
Expected Response: Flowers.
Teacher Confirms/Expands: Yes! Beautiful flowers grow in gardens in spring.

text:
TG_SOP_Level3-4
Standard Operating Procedure
Teacher’s Guide Generation
Level 3–4 • A1+ to A2 • Reading to Learn
System Rules
Part 1: Lesson Overview
Purpose: Provide a concise reference that allows the teacher to understand the lesson’s scope, goals, and curriculum position at a glance before teaching.
Required Fields
Include the following fields in a two-column table:
Key Objectives
Begin with “Students will be able to…” List 4–5 objectives that map directly to the Reading Strategy, Reading Skill, core vocabulary, and language focus for this book.
Question Level Guide
Include a fixed reference table immediately after Key Objectives. This table explains the [L0] / [L1] / [L2] tags that appear on all comprehension questions throughout the TG. The table must appear in every TG so teachers understand the tags before they begin teaching.
Lesson Flow at a Glance
Include a summary table showing all stages, their focus, and time allocation. Use stage headers (PRE-READING, DURING READING, POST-READING) as sub-rows within the table to aid navigation.
Part 2: Pre-Reading
Time: 6 minutes total
Warm-up • 3 minutes
Purpose: Activate prior knowledge and create a genuine reason to read. Connect to the book’s theme without revealing the story.
Phonics Awareness • 3 minutes
Purpose: Brief awareness check only. Students at Level 3–4 have solid phonics foundations. This section builds sound recognition, not decoding from scratch.
Part 3: During Reading
Time: 25 minutes total • Picture Walk (5 min) + Detailed Reading (15 min) + Rereading for Fluency (5 min)
Step 1: Picture Walk • 5 minutes
Purpose: Build meaning, set up the reading skill and strategy, and introduce core vocabulary naturally through pictures before text is read aloud.
Step 2: Detailed Reading • 15 minutes
Purpose: Read the full text with comprehension focus. Build the Reading Skill and Reading Strategy through structured Pause Points placed at key moments in the text.
Reading Routine
Teacher reads aloud (model fluency and expression) → Students echo read → Students choral read
Maintain this routine at every page. Do not skip any page.
Step 3: Rereading for Fluency • 5 minutes
Purpose: Build reading automaticity, expression, and confidence through purposeful repeated reading.
Part 4: Post-Reading
Time: 29 minutes total • Reading Check (10 min) + Worksheet Practice (15 min) + Lesson Close (4 min)
Reading Check • 10 minutes
Worksheet Practice • 15 minutes
Purpose: Consolidate vocabulary, language patterns, reading comprehension, and the Text-to-Self or Text-to-World connection through structured activities.
Lesson Close • 4 minutes
Part 5: Portfolio / Extension Tasks
Purpose: Provide 3 differentiated task options for students to demonstrate understanding beyond the lesson. Select one based on available time and student need.
Part 6: Independent Reading (Optional)
Part 7: Quality Checklist
Before finalising any TG generated using this SOP, verify every item below:
Appendix: Key Differences from Level 1–2 SOP
This table summarises how the L3–4 SOP differs from the lower-level SOP to help editors and reviewers understand the design rationale.
⚠️ These rules apply to every generated TG without exception.

100% English Only: The entire TG must be written in English. Do not use any other language anywhere in the output.
No Meta-Commentary: Do not print internal reasoning, selection labels, or option names. Output only the final TG content.
No Vocabulary Pre-Teaching: Do not include a Vocabulary Preview section. All core vocabulary is introduced naturally in context during the Picture Walk and Detailed Reading.
Readability: Use clear line breaks between sections, steps, and paragraphs. Avoid dense blocks of text.
Curriculum Alignment: Every question, pause point, and activity must align directly to the Reading Strategy and Reading Skill specified in the syllabus for this book.
Bold Standards — Apply bold to:
Section headings and sub-headings
Instructional tags: Teacher Says:, Teacher Asks:

title: Storybook Readers Literacy Style Guide
text:
Storybook Readers Literacy Style Guide
Student-Facing and Teacher-Facing Standards
OVERARCHING RULE

Proofreaders must apply two standards depending on the content type:
Content Type | Standard
Storybook text (student-facing) | Literacy style guide (pedagogical English)
Instructional and teacher-facing text | APA 7th Edition (adapted for clarity)
DO NOT CORRECT (CRITICAL)

The following are intentional and must not be corrected:
Allowed in story text:
Sentence fragments (e.g., "Sweet." "My mother.")
Repetition
Simple sentence frames
Limited vocabulary reuse
Short, predictable sentences

These features support early literacy development, including decoding, fluency, and comprehension.
STORY TEXT RULES (STUDENT-FACING)

3.1 Capitalization
Do:
Capitalize the first word of each sentence
Capitalize proper nouns only

Do not:
Capitalize common nouns unnecessarily

3.2 Punctuation
Use:
Periods (.)
Question marks (?) for questions

Specific rules:
Keep punctuation simple and consistent
Contractions are allowed (e.g., "It’s my grandma.")

3.3 Sentence Acceptability
Accept:
Sentence fragments when intentional
One idea per line
Repetitive structures

Flag:
Grammar errors that are not intentional
Missing punctuation
Incorrect word usage

3.4 Spacing and Line Breaks
Check:
No extra spaces between words
No space before punctuation (e.g., "...grandma." not "...grandma .")

Flag:
Broken phrases across lines (e.g., "My / grandma." or "grandma / .")

3.5 Questions in Story
Format:
Capitalize the first word
End with a question mark

Examples:
What is it?
Who is older?
VOCABULARY RULES

4.1 Vocabulary Lists
Rule:
All vocabulary must be in base form (lemma)
Do:
walk
read
friend

Do not:
walks
reading
friends

4.2 Formatting
Do:
Use lowercase unless the word is a proper noun
Do not include punctuation at the end
Use consistent separators (commas or line breaks)

Flag:
Trailing commas
Mixed capitalization
Inconsistent formatting
INSTRUCTIONAL PAGES (APA 7th EDITION, ADAPTED)

Applies to:
Before Reading
During Reading
After Reading
Strategy pages
SoR labels

5.1 Headings
Rule:
Use Title Case with quotation marks
Correct:
“Before Reading” Strategies
“During Reading” Strategies
“After Reading” Strategies

Incorrect:
before reading strategies

5.2 Strategy Names
Use Title Case:
Picture Walk
Preview High-Frequency Words
Text-to-Self Connection

5.3 Sentence Style
Do:
Use complete sentences
Use clear, direct instructional language
Use quotation marks and italics for exact teacher phrasing

Do not:
Use fragments in instructional text
Use overly complex sentences
“This story is about hair care. How do you take care of your hair?”

5.4 Lists and Bullets
Do:
Maintain parallel verb structure
Use consistent grammar forms

Correct:
Look at the pictures
Talk about what you see
Predict the story

Incorrect:
Look at the pictures
Talking about what you see
Prediction

5.5 SoR Labels
Use consistent terminology. Do not change unless incorrect.
Approved examples:
Language Comprehension
Fluency Foundations
Long-Term Word Learning

Flag only if:
Misspelled
Inconsistent formatting

PRACTICE AND RESPONSE SECTIONS

6.1 Sentence Writing
Check:
Correct capitalization
Correct punctuation
Natural English phrasing

6.2 Questions
Must:
Start with a capital letter
End with a question mark

LAYOUT AND FORMATTING

Proofreaders should flag but not fix:
Repeated page numbers
Misaligned text
Spacing inconsistencies
Visual layout issues
COMMON ERRORS TO FLAG

Language errors:
Missing punctuation
Incorrect capitalization
Incorrect word forms in vocabulary lists
Grammar errors that are not intentional

Formatting errors:
Space before punctuation
Broken lines or phrases
Inconsistent headings

PROOFREADER CHECKLIST

Story Text:
Capitalization is correct
Punctuation is correct
No spacing errors
No broken phrases

Vocabulary:
Base form only
Lowercase
No punctuation errors

Instructional Text:
Title Case headings
Quotation marks around exact teacher phrasing only
Complete sentences
